steps.title: Identify the contrast between actual and predicted
steps.content: The stem tells us P6 gave equal ratings to abstract and cubist paintings. But the graph shows markedly different correlations for the two styles: abstract at 0.42 (higher) and cubist at 0.27 (lower). This gap reveals something about what the model predicted.

steps.title: Infer the model's prediction from the correlation mismatch
steps.content:
When two variables have different correlations across categories—while one variable (P6's actual ratings) stayed equal—the other variable (the model's predictions) must have differed between them. If the model predicted equal average enjoyment for both styles, the correlation patterns should match. Since they don't, the model must have predicted unequal ratings. Choices C and D specify a direction; choice B claims the opposite direction. Choice A states simply that the model predicted a difference, which is the logical inference: the model predicted P6's ratings would differ, even though P6 actually gave equal ratings.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
